original_hypothesis.title: Proving Window Indefinitely Extended via Repeated Pause/Unpause by chain_pauser
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Both pauseProving(false) (via LibProving) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt. A chain_pauser who repeatedly toggles provingPaused (true->false) or the contract pause state resets lastUnpausedAt, extending the assigned prover's exclusive proving window indefinitely and preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If the assigned prover colludes with chain_pauser, they can monopolize the proving window indefinitely, preventing honest provers from submitting alternative proofs and potentially stalling the verification chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) for window calculation; pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt

preconditions: chain_pauser role is set (resolved via AddressManager)
Assigned prover intentionally does not prove within the original window

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: LibProving.sol lines 73-78 (pauseProving resets lastUnpausedAt) and lines 303-316 (_checkProverPermission window calc)
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: lastUnpausedAt used as lower bound of proving window start; can be repeatedly reset
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No cap on how many times or how late lastUnpausedAt can be reset relative to block proposal time
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum proving window duration anchored to block proposal time

analysis_questions: How frequently can chain_pauser toggle the pause state?
Does the cooldown window in verification also use lastUnpausedAt, compounding the delay?
Can this be used to permanently prevent verification of specific blocks?
validation_requirements: Confirm that repeated pause/unpause resets lastUnpausedAt each time
Verify that max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) produces an ever-extending window
Test that non-assigned provers are permanently blocked while window is extended

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Block B proposed with assigned prover P at time T. 2) Proving window = T + 60 minutes (SGX tier). 3) P does not prove. 4) At T+59min, chain_pauser calls pauseProving(true) then pauseProving(false), resetting lastUnpausedAt to T+59min. 5) New window: max(T, T+59min) + 60min = T+119min. 6) Repeat before each window expiry. 7) No other prover can ever submit proof for first transition of B.
prerequisites: Collusion between chain_pauser and assigned prover

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Block verification stalled; provers' bonds locked; L2 chain progression halted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All L2 users and honest provers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner removes chain_pauser role or upgrades contract

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for proving window extension via repeated pause toggling

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Two pauseProving calls per extension (~50K gas each)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring ProvingPaused events frequency

validation_result.mechanism: Proving window extension via pause toggling
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious or compromised chain_pauser can indefinitely extend the exclusive proving window for assigned provers and reset verification cooldowns, stalling block finality.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `_checkProverPermission` function in `LibProving` calculates the exclusive proving window for the assigned prover using `max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow * 60`. The `lastUnpausedAt` state variable is updated to `block.timestamp` every time the contract is unpaused via `TaikoL1.unpause()` or `TaikoL1.pauseProving(false)`. If a `chain_pauser` repeatedly toggles the pause state before the proving window expires, `lastUnpausedAt` is continuously refreshed to the current timestamp. This ensures that `max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt)` is always close to `block.timestamp`, keeping `inProvingWindow` true indefinitely. Consequently, the assigned prover retains their exclusive right to submit the first transition proof, preventing any other provers from stepping in. Furthermore, this global reset of `lastUnpausedAt` also affects the cooldown window in `LibVerifying.verifyBlocks`, meaning a single pause toggle resets the cooldown timer for all previously proven blocks, severely delaying chain finality.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence directly supports the hypothesis. `LibProving.pauseProving` updates `_state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt = uint64(block.timestamp)` when `_pause` is false. `LibProving._checkProverPermission` uses this value to calculate `inProvingWindow`. There is no limit on how many times the pause state can be toggled, nor is there a maximum cap on the window extension relative to the block's original proposal time (`ts.timestamp`).

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When unpausing (`_pause == false`), `lastUnpausedAt` is set to the current `block.timestamp`. This can be triggered repeatedly by toggling the pause state.

The `inProvingWindow` calculation uses `max(_ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt)`. By repeatedly updating `lastUnpausedAt` to the current time, `inProvingWindow` remains true, locking out all other provers from submitting the first transition.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The `chain_pauser` calls `pauseProving(true)` followed by `pauseProving(false)`. This updates `_state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt` to the current `block.timestamp`. When an honest prover attempts to call `proveBlock` for an unproven block (`_tid == 1`, `_ts.tier == 0`), `_checkProverPermission` evaluates `inProvingWindow` using the newly updated `lastUnpausedAt`. Since `lastUnpausedAt + provingWindow * 60 >= block.timestamp` is true, the honest prover is rejected by `revert L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER()`. This can be repeated indefinitely.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid. A compromised or malicious `chain_pauser` can collude with an assigned prover. The assigned prover intentionally delays submitting a proof. Before the `provingWindow` expires, the `chain_pauser` toggles the proving pause state, resetting `lastUnpausedAt`. This extends the assigned prover's exclusive window by another full `provingWindow` duration. Honest provers are continuously locked out. Additionally, this action resets the cooldown window for all other blocks, stalling the entire verification process.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High impact on liveness and finality. It allows a privileged role to indefinitely stall block verification and extend exclusive proving windows without keeping the system in a permanently paused state. It also resets the cooldown for all previously proven blocks, delaying their finality.
recommended_mitigations: Instead of resetting `lastUnpausedAt` to `block.timestamp`, track the total accumulated paused time and add it to the window calculations.
Alternatively, cap the maximum extension a window can receive (e.g., `max(ts.timestamp + maxExtension, lastUnpausedAt)`).
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TaikoL1 and configure a `chain_pauser`. 2. Propose a block with an assigned prover. 3. Wait until just before the `provingWindow` expires. 4. Have the `chain_pauser` call `pauseProving(true)` then `pauseProving(false)`. 5. Attempt to prove the block from a different (honest) prover address and observe it revert with `L1_NOT_ASSIGNED_PROVER`. 6. Repeat steps 3-5 to demonstrate indefinite extension.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol considers the `chain_pauser` completely trusted to not abuse the pause toggle. However, the side effect of resetting all cooldowns makes this a significant architectural flaw regardless of trust assumptions.
additional_testing_needed: Test the impact of `pauseProving` toggling on the `verifyBlocks` cooldown window to confirm the secondary DoS effect on block finality.
